The problem with secure and confidential data is that if leaked to AI models,
it can lead to data breaches and privacy violations bearing substantial legal
and reputational risks. We must remember that it is **our responsibility to**:

1. control what data is shared with the AI.
2. review and curate the context AI uses.
3. properly anonymize, aggregate, or simulate data before sharing with AI.
4. follow organizational policies and legal requirements for responsible
   AI use ([ai/](https://ai.worldbank.org/)).

::: callout-important
Please familiarize yourself with the [World Bank's AI Use Policy](https://ai.worldbank.org/policy) and [Responsible AI Guidelines](https://ai.worldbank.org/guidelines) to ensure compliance and responsible use of AI in your work.
:::

### Key data exposure channels

In general, **AI does not load raw data into context as is**. However,
as it develops code to understand the data, it can potentially
propose doing things that lead to data exposure. Based on the literature,[^1]
the most common channels of data exposure are:

[^1]: Yan, B., Li, K., Xu, M., Dong, Y., Zhang, Y., Ren, Z., & Cheng, X. (2024). *On Protecting the Data Privacy of Large Language Models (LLMs): A Survey*. arXiv:2403.05156. <https://arxiv.org/abs/2403.05156>

1. **user-written prompts that include sensitive information**,
2. **AI suggests code that prints raw data in the console**,
3. **AI instructs the IDE to read text files or load them into context**,
4. **AI proposes command-line code that searches for specific text in data files (e.g. `.csv` files) or loads them into context**.

In any of these cases, sensitive data becomes part of the context that AI uses
to generate code and outputs, and thus **there is always a risk of data leakage**.

::: callout-note

In principle and depending on the AI provider, each chat session with AI is an
isolated instance of a foundation model. Data shared with it may not be used
for training, but it may be retained for a certain period of time. However,
**every model provider has different**
**policies on data usage, retention, and training**. If needed, please
familiarize yourself with:

1. [Positron Assistant Provider Privacy Information](https://positron.posit.co/assistant-provider-info.html),
2. [Responsible use of GitHub Copilot inline suggestions](https://docs.github.com/en/copilot/responsible-use/copilot-code-completion), and
3. [Responsible use of GitHub Copilot Chat in your IDE](https://docs.github.com/en/copilot/responsible-use/chat-in-your-ide).

:::

### Remedies and best practices to safeguard data {#remedies-safeguard-data}

- [**Be careful about what you write in prompts**](/methods/_safeguard-data-details.qmd#be-careful) — share variable names, types, and summary statistics instead of raw data values; never include passwords or personally identifiable information.
- [**Instruct AI to NOT read specific files**](/methods/_safeguard-data-details.qmd#instruct-ai-not-read-files) — use system instructions (`.github/copilot-instructions.md`, `claude.md`) and `.gitignore` to prevent AI from accessing sensitive data files.
- [**Instruct AI to NOT print raw data in the console**](/methods/_safeguard-data-details.qmd#instruct-ai-not-print-data) — add standing instructions that forbid code from displaying individual-level observations; allow only aggregated statistics and regression results.
- [**Use metadata and move data outside of the working folder**](/methods/_safeguard-data-details.qmd#use-metadata) — generate lightweight metadata files (variable names, types, summary stats) and keep raw data in a separate location so it never enters AI context.
- [**Anonymize data before sharing**](/methods/_safeguard-data-details.qmd#data-anonymization) — apply de-identification techniques (suppression, generalization, noise addition, pseudonymization) and verify protection levels (k-anonymity, l-diversity, t-closeness) using dedicated packages such as `sdcMicro` or `deident` (R), `pycanon` or `Faker` (Python).
